section.title: Judgment for Israel's Sin
section.sectionRationale: The harvest festivals were times of joy, but Hosea forbids rejoicing: the joy of the threshing floor is the joy of a harlot's wages. This oracle traces exile that undoes the exodus, a religion that will fail, and a love story turned to grief, God found Israel like grapes in the wilderness, but they gave themselves to Shame.
section.thesis: Israel's harvest joy is hollow because their prosperity was bought by spiritual harlotry; judgment means exile, the collapse of their worship, and even the withdrawal of God's love from a people who consecrated themselves to Shame.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the forbidden festival and coming exile; the days of reckoning when prophet and people are ensnared; and the tender memory of Baal-peor turned to a lament over withered fruit and wandering.

units.label: Do not rejoice: the harlot's wages
units.note: no home in the land of the LORD

verses.text: Do not rejoice, O Israel, with exultation like the nations, for you have played the {{zanah|harlot}} against your God; you have made love for hire on every threshing floor.
The threshing floor and winepress will not feed them, and the new wine will fail them.
They will not remain in the land of the LORD; Ephraim will return to Egypt and eat unclean food in Assyria.

They will not pour out wine offerings to the LORD, and their sacrifices will not please Him, but will be to them like the bread of mourners; all who eat will be defiled. For their bread will be for themselves; it will not enter the house of the LORD.
What will you do on the appointed day, on the day of the LORD’s feast?

units.label: The days of reckoning
units.note: the prophet a fool, the watchman ensnared

verses.text: For even if they flee destruction, Egypt will gather them and Memphis will bury them. Their precious silver will be taken over by thistles, and thorns will overrun their tents.

The days of punishment have come; the days of retribution have arrived, let Israel know it. The prophet is called a fool, and the inspired man insane, because of the greatness of your iniquity and hostility.
The prophet is Ephraim’s watchman, along with my God, yet the snare of the fowler lies on all his paths. Hostility is in the house of his God!
They have deeply corrupted themselves as in the days of Gibeah; He will remember their guilt; He will punish their sins.

units.label: From grapes in the wilderness to Shame
units.note: a love remembered, a fruit withered

verses.text: I found Israel like grapes in the wilderness. I saw your fathers as the firstfruits of the fig tree in its first season. But they went to Baal-peor, and consecrated themselves to {{bosheth|Shame}}; so they became as detestable as the thing they loved.
Ephraim’s glory will fly away like a bird, with no birth, no pregnancy, and no conception.
Even if they raise their children, I will bereave them of each one. Yes, woe be to them when I turn away from them!
I have seen Ephraim, like Tyre, planted in a meadow. But Ephraim will bring out his children for slaughter.
Give them, O LORD, what will You give? Give them wombs that miscarry and breasts that dry up!
All their evil appears at Gilgal, for there I hated them. I will drive them from My house for the wickedness of their deeds. I will no longer love them; all their leaders are rebellious.
Ephraim is struck down; their root is withered; they cannot bear fruit. Even if they bear children, I will slay the darlings of their wombs.
My God will {{maas|reject}} them because they have not obeyed Him; and they shall be wanderers among the nations.

zanah.gloss: To play the harlot, to be a prostitute, to be unfaithful. It is Hosea's central image for Israel's covenant betrayal, drawn from the prophet's own marriage. Here the harvest gained through fertility rites at the threshing floor is exposed as a harlot's wages, so their joy is really shame.

bosheth.gloss: Shame, disgrace. It became a deliberate nickname for Baal, so that consecrating themselves to Shame means devoting themselves to the shameful idol at Baal-peor. The word carries a bitter irony: what Israel embraced as glory was in truth their disgrace.

maas.gloss: To reject, to spurn, to cast off. The same word ran through chapter 4, where Israel rejected knowledge and God rejected them as priests. Here it reaches its darkest point, My God will reject them, showing the grave consequence of persistent disobedience, yet within a book that ends in healing.

basic.context: Harvest was a season of feasting, but Hosea says do not celebrate. The grain and wine Israel enjoyed came through worship of fertility gods, so their prosperity is really a harlot's pay. Judgment means exile: they will leave the LORD's land, eat unclean food in Assyria, and their sacrifices will be useless. God even calls the prophet a fool in their eyes and warns of days of reckoning. Then comes a heartbreaking memory: God found Israel as delightful as grapes in the desert, but they gave themselves to Baal, to Shame, and became as vile as the idol they loved.
basic.hermeneutics: This is among the darkest oracles in Hosea, and its harshest lines, including the prayer for barren wombs, must be read as prophetic lament and judgment within the flow of the book, not as God's final word; chapters 11 and 14 show mercy prevailing. The chapter interprets Israel's material blessings through a spiritual lens: what looked like fruitfulness was fed by idolatry.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is dominated by zanah, to play the harlot, Israel's covenant betrayal. Baal is named bosheth, Shame, so Israel consecrated itself to disgrace. And the grim climax uses maas, to reject: My God will reject them, the same word by which Israel first rejected knowledge in chapter 4.
basic.typology: Israel found like grapes in the wilderness, then turning barren and rejected, points by contrast to Christ, the true vine who bears the fruit Israel could not, and the true and faithful Israel who is never rejected but is received by the Father for His people's sake. The exile that undoes the exodus points to the deeper exile of sin from which Christ brings His people home.
basic.christ: God found Israel like grapes in the wilderness, but their root withered and they could bear no fruit. Jesus is the true vine, and those who abide in Him bear the fruit Israel never could. Israel was rejected because they had not obeyed; Christ, the faithful Son, obeyed perfectly and bore the rejection of the cross, My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, so that His people would never be finally cast off. The wanderers among the nations foreshadow all who are far from God, whom the Good Shepherd seeks and gathers home.
basic.god: God is holy and cannot be bribed by festival joy built on idolatry; He sees the truth behind the celebration. His judgment here is severe, even to withdrawing His love and driving them from His house, showing that grace persistently spurned meets real consequences. Yet the aching memory of grapes in the wilderness reveals a God who loved His people from the first and grieves their ruin, which is why the book does not end here.
basic.crossReferences: John 15 presents Christ as the true vine in whom His people bear fruit. Numbers 25 records the sin at Baal-peor that Hosea recalls. Romans 11 speaks of Israel's rejection and the hope of their being received again.
basic.oneStory: Chapter 9 laments a people once delightful as desert grapes who consecrated themselves to Shame and now face rejection and exile. The Bible answers this grief in Christ, the true vine and faithful Israel, who bears the fruit they could not, endures the rejection they deserved, and gathers the wanderers home.

context.literary: A festival oracle that inverts joy into lament. It moves from a ban on rejoicing, through the days of reckoning, to a poignant historical retrospect, grapes in the wilderness, that collapses into images of barrenness and withered roots. The tone is grief as much as anger.
context.historical: Harvest feasts at threshing floors were bound up with Canaanite fertility worship. Baal-peor recalls Israel's sin in Numbers 25, and Gibeah recalls the atrocity of Judges 19. The threats of Egypt and Assyria reflect the looming Assyrian exile that would scatter the northern kingdom among the nations.
context.canonical: The vine and firstfruit imagery links Israel's story to Christ the true vine in John 15 and to Paul's olive tree in Romans 11. The theme of wandering exile runs from the curses of Deuteronomy 28 to its answer in the gospel, where the scattered are gathered in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To strip away the false joy of a prosperity gained through idolatry, to announce the exile that will undo the exodus, and to grieve over a people once precious to God who ruined themselves at Baal-peor, that they might feel the weight of their betrayal.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes judgment and voices lament, including the harsh prayer of verse 14; it does not prescribe such prayers as a model for us. What endures is the warning against worship-fueled prosperity divorced from God and the call to bear true fruit.

debates.question: How should we read the severe prayer of verse 14, give them wombs that miscarry?

views.label: Prophetic lament handing judgment to God
views.case: Hosea, overwhelmed by Israel's guilt, asks God to give what their sin has earned rather than prolong it, a grieved surrender to divine justice rather than personal vindictiveness.

views.label: Ironic echo of the coming judgment
views.case: The prayer voices the very barrenness God has already announced, dramatizing the horror of exile so the people grasp what their idolatry brings.

debates.bearing: Either way the verse is judgment language within a book that moves toward healing; it must be read alongside chapters 11 and 14, where God's mercy overrules His wrath in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The oracle is saturated with zanah, to play the harlot, the word that names Israel's betrayal and unmasks their harvest joy as a prostitute's pay earned on every threshing floor. At its aching center, Baal is called bosheth, Shame, so that in going to Baal-peor Israel consecrated itself to disgrace and became as detestable as the idol it loved. The chapter ends with maas, to reject: My God will reject them because they would not obey. This is the same verb by which Israel rejected knowledge in chapter 4, now turned back upon them, yet within a book whose last chapter promises healing.

typology.type: Israel found like grapes but bearing no fruit
typology.fulfillment: Christ the true vine, in whom His people bear the fruit Israel could not, John 15:1-5.
Christ the obedient Son who bore rejection in their place so they would not be finally cast off, Mark 15:34.
The lost whom the Good Shepherd seeks and gathers home, Luke 19:10 and John 10:16.

study.christ: God found Israel like grapes in the wilderness, but their root withered until they could bear no fruit. Jesus declares Himself the true vine, and those who abide in Him bear the fruit Israel never could. Israel was rejected because they had not obeyed, but Christ, the faithful Son, obeyed to the end and took upon Himself the rejection they deserved, crying out My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, so that His people would never be finally cast off. The wanderers among the nations picture every sinner far from God, and the Good Shepherd comes to seek and gather them, turning exile into homecoming.
study.god: The LORD is holy and clear-eyed, refusing to be flattered by festival joy that grows out of idolatry. His judgment in this chapter reaches its darkest, even to withdrawing His love and driving His people from His house, a sober witness that grace spurned again and again meets real consequences. Yet under the anger runs grief: the memory of grapes in the wilderness reveals a God who loved His people from the beginning and mourns their self-ruin. That love is exactly why the book cannot end in rejection but presses on to healing.

crossReferences.note: Christ the true vine bears in His people the fruit Israel failed to bear.
The sin at Baal-peor that Hosea recalls, when Israel yoked itself to Baal.
God has not utterly rejected His people; a remnant is kept by grace, pointing beyond Hosea's judgment.
The Son of Man came to seek and save the lost, gathering the wanderers home.

study.oneStory: Chapter 9 laments a people once delightful as desert grapes who gave themselves to Shame and now face rejection and exile. The gospel answers this grief in Christ, the true vine and faithful Israel, who bears the fruit they could not, endures the rejection they deserved, and gathers the wandering home to God.

quiz.q: Why does Hosea forbid Israel to rejoice at harvest?

options.text: Their prosperity was gained through spiritual harlotry, so the joy is really a harlot's wages.
options.note: Verse 1 ties the harvest joy to unfaithful worship at the threshing floor.

options.text: Rejoicing at harvest is always sinful.
options.note: The problem is the idolatry behind this joy, not celebration itself.

options.text: The harvest was simply too small to enjoy.
options.note: The issue is the source of the abundance, not its size.

options.text: God forbids all feasting forever.
options.note: God gave Israel feasts; here He exposes joy corrupted by idolatry.

quiz.q: What does judgment mean for Israel in this chapter?

options.text: Exile from the LORD's land, the failure of their worship, and scattering among the nations.
options.note: Verses 3 to 6 and 17 describe exile and wandering.

options.text: A short delay before renewed blessing.
options.note: The chapter announces deep judgment, not a brief pause.

options.text: Only a mild rebuke with no consequences.
options.note: The consequences here are severe: exile and rejection.

options.text: Victory over Assyria and Egypt.
options.note: The opposite; they fall under these powers.

quiz.q: What does the memory of grapes in the wilderness reveal?

options.text: God once delighted in Israel, and grieves that they gave themselves to Shame and became like the idol they loved.
options.note: Verse 10 recalls God's early delight, then their fall at Baal-peor.

options.text: That Israel was always worthless to God.
options.note: God found them precious like desert grapes; the tragedy is their fall.

options.text: That God cares only about agriculture.
options.note: The grapes are an image of God's delight in His people.

options.text: That idols are harmless.
options.note: The idol made them detestable; idolatry is ruinous.

quiz.q: How does barren, fruitless Israel point to Christ?

options.text: Against a withered, fruitless people stands Christ the true vine, in whom His people bear real fruit.
options.note: John 15 presents Christ as the vine who makes His people fruitful.

options.text: It shows fruitfulness does not matter to God.
options.note: God desires fruit; Christ supplies what Israel lacked.

options.text: It predicts a literal grape shortage.
options.note: The image is spiritual fruitfulness, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It has no link to Christ.
options.note: Christ the true vine directly answers Israel's barrenness.

quiz.q: How should the harshest lines of this chapter be read?

options.text: As real judgment and lament within a book that moves toward healing in chapters 11 and 14.
options.note: The severity is genuine but not God's final word in Hosea.

options.text: As God's eternal, unchangeable last word over His people.
options.note: The book itself moves beyond this to mercy and healing.

options.text: As proof that God never shows mercy.
options.note: Hosea 11 and 14 overflow with mercy.

options.text: As a model prayer to pray against enemies.
options.note: These are prophetic lament and judgment, not a template for us.

questions: Israel's joy was hollow because of what lay behind it. Is there any prosperity or pleasure in your life whose true source you would rather not examine?
God found Israel delightful, but they became like the idol they loved. What do you tend to become like when you give your heart to something other than God?
The people treated the prophet as a fool for speaking truth. When have you been tempted to dismiss God's word because it exposed your sin?
Christ the true vine gives fruit that we cannot produce on our own. In what area do you need to abide in Him rather than strive in your own strength?
This dark chapter is not Hosea's last word; healing is coming. How does knowing where the story ends help you sit honestly with seasons of God's discipline?

translationNotes.note: The BSB prints an em dash after the days of retribution have arrived; it is rendered here as a comma per house style.
The prophet is Ephraim's watchman, along with my God may also be rendered the prophet is the watchman over Ephraim, the people of my God.
Shame renders bosheth, a deliberate substitute for the name Baal, so consecrating themselves to Shame means devoting themselves to the shameful idol at Baal-peor.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hosea 9:1-17, with poetic lines joined into running verses. Em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas per house style.
